steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure before and after the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: “Recordings of electrical activity in the brain,” is a noun phrase (the subject) followed by a comma. After the blank: the sentence must continue with material that connects this subject to the phrase “increased activity...” The blank must supply a subject and verb for the main clause.

steps.title: Determine which choice creates a logical appositive and correct subject-verb relationship
steps.content: In choice A, “electrograms” is an appositive that renames “Recordings”—an electrogram is literally a recording of electrical activity—and “show” serves as the verb: “Recordings of electrical activity in the brain, electrograms show that while responding to hypothetical match scenarios, the most highly skilled soccer players have increased activity...” This makes semantic sense: the recordings show that players have increased activity in suppressive motor areas. The phrase “while responding to hypothetical match scenarios” also provides necessary context for the brain activity. In choices B and D, “responses” is an appositive for “Recordings,” which is nonsensical—responses are not recordings. In choice C, the structure inverts the logic: “the most highly skilled soccer players...have electrograms,” which falsely suggests players possess electrograms rather than being subjects of the recorded activity. That matches choice A — A is correct.
